summary: Gruber examines Google's staggering 52% share of global digital ad spending and argues it is fundamentally unjustified relative to Google's actual share of user attention. He draws analogies to cable companies or printing presses capturing most of the revenue meant for content creators. He connects Google's outsized revenue to user tracking, arguing that privacy-invasive surveillance is what enabled this disproportionate take. He concludes that a privacy-respecting internet may be incompatible with Google and Facebook maintaining their current dominance, comparing ad tracking to carbon emissions as a form of profitable pollution whose reckoning has arrived.
key_points: Google accounts for 52% of global digital ad spending and controls 40% of open-internet ad money flow, figures far exceeding its share of user attention
Ad revenue should be roughly commensurate with attention share, but Google has inserted itself as a middleman taking far more than a middleman's cut
User tracking is the mechanism that enabled Google and Facebook to capture such a disproportionate share of ad spending
A genuine shift toward online privacy may be structurally incompatible with Google and Facebook's current business models
Even halving Google's ad share to 25% would still represent an amazing business, but would feel like a collapse compared to the status quo
money_quotes: At a certain level it just doesn't feel justified that Google should be involved with this much of the world's advertising spend.
Google has inserted itself into the middle, yet is taking far more than a middleman-sized share of the money.
It's like finding out that half the money spent on TV advertising wasn't going to the channels where the commercials appeared, but to the cable companies.
The desire to know as much information as possible about the audience for advertising has always been the Pied Piper lure of the industry.

I think it's possible that we can have a world where our online activities are far more private, or a world where Google and Facebook can maintain their current outsized share of worldwide ad spending, but not both.

A world where Google sees, say, 25 percent of the world's ad spending sounds like an amazing business, in principle. Unless you're comparing it to the world we're in today, where they see 50 percent — then 25 percent looks like a collapse.

Privacy-invasive user tracking is to Google and Facebook what carbon emissions are to fossil fuel companies — a form of highly profitable pollution that for a very long time few people in the mainstream cared about, but now, seemingly suddenly, very many care about quite a bit.
key_insight: Google's dominance of digital advertising is built not on proportional attention share but on privacy-invasive tracking, and a genuine privacy reckoning may be fundamentally incompatible with that business model.
